coredata.dc:title: Surface charge-dependent osteogenic behaviors of edge-functionalized graphene quantum dots

coredata.dc:description: Understanding the relationship between stem cell’ osteogenic capacity and physicochemical properties of nanomaterials is essential for designing new tissue engineering materials capable of regulating stem cell fate. It has reported that the surface potential of nanomaterials affects cell behavior, however, the underlying mechanism is still controversial and surface charge was not the only variable in the studies performed. In this work, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used as a simplified model for single-factor study for the effect of surface charge on osteogenic differentiation, by changing the surface charge of GQD only while keeping all other variables constant. Notably, it was found that the positively charged GQD+ exerted no impact on osteogenic differentiation, while the negatively charged GQD‾ could significantly enhance osteogenic differentiation of human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) by activating the BMP/Smad signaling pathway. The negatively charged GQD‾ was then encapsulated in gelatin methacrylamide (GelMA) hydrogel via a UV light-activated photopolymerization in the presence of photoinitiator, obtaining an 3D injectable GQD‾/GelMA hydrogel scaffold. A significantly accelerated bone regeneration induced by GQD‾/GelMA hydrogel scaffolds was observed in the mouse calvarial defect model. The systematic study of the effects of surface charge on the stem cell behavior could provide much inspiration for the tissue engineering application of nanomaterials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Scheme showing the synthesis process of three types of GQDs with different surface charge paterns. (b) TEM images of GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾, the insets are lateral size distribution diagrams. (c) HRTEM images of GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾.

(a) UV–vis absorption (ABS), PL, and excitation (PLE) spectra of GQD‾, GQD +/ ‾, and GQD+. (b) PL spectra of GQD‾, GQD +/ ‾, and GQD+ excited at different wavelengths. (c) Photostability test of GQD‾, GQD +/ ‾, and GQD+ under 6-h continuous radiation using a 100 W xenon lamp. (d) Dependence of PL intensity of GQD‾, GQD +/ ‾, and GQD+ on pH values.
(a) Zeta potential (n = 3), (b) XRD, (c) Raman, and (d-f) FT-IR spectra of GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾.

(a-c) ARS stained images (a-b) and calcium nodule images (c) of hMSCs cultured with GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾ for 14 days, respectively. Scale bars, 200 μm. (d) Quantification results of ARS staining (n = 3, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001). (e) ALP activity of hMSCs (n = 3, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001).

(a, c) The protein levels (a) and quantitative analysis (c) of osteogenic-related proteins (COL-1, OCN, β-catenin, and RUNX2) of hMSCs cultured with GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾ for 14 days (n = 3, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001). GAPDH is used as a loading control. (b, d) The protein levels (b) and quantitative analysis (d) of BMP/Smad pathway-related proteins (BMP, p-Smad1/5, and Smad 1/5/8) and MAPK pathway-related proteins (p-ERK) of hMSCs cultured with GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾ for 14 days (n = 3, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001). (e) Schematic illustration of the enhanced osteogenic differentiation by GQD‾ through BMP/Smad signaling pathway.

(a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels. (b) Images of GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels at varied GQD‾ concentration (0, 100, 300 μg mL−1) before and after UV light irradiation. (c) SEM image of GQD‾ encapsulated GelMA hydrogel scaffolds. (d) Swelling kinetics of the GelMA and GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels in DI water at 37 °C (n = 3). (e) The in vitro biodegradation of GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels in DI water, PBS or DMEM (n = 3). (f) The compression modulus evaluation of GelMA and GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels (n = 3). (g) Alizarin red S stained images and calcium nodule images of hMSCs cultured on GelMA hydrogels with or without GQD‾ modification. Scale bars, 200 μm. (h) Micro-CT images calvarial defects treated with GelMA hydrogels with or without GQD‾ modification. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Overall scheme of the fabrication of a 3D GQD‾/GelMA hydrogel scaffold by using a UV light-activated photopolymerization, by which negatively charged GQD‾ was incorporated in the porous hydrogel system and slowly released at the bone defect area to promote bone regeneration in situ.

head.ce:title: Surface charge-dependent osteogenic behaviors of edge-functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Understanding the relationship between stem cell’ osteogenic capacity and physicochemical properties of nanomaterials is essential for designing new tissue engineering materials capable of regulating stem cell fate. It has reported that the surface potential of nanomaterials affects cell behavior, however, the underlying mechanism is still controversial and surface charge was not the only variable in the studies performed. In this work, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used as a simplified model for single-factor study for the effect of surface charge on osteogenic differentiation, by changing the surface charge of GQD only while keeping all other variables constant. Notably, it was found that the positively charged GQD+ exerted no impact on osteogenic differentiation, while the negatively charged GQD‾ could significantly enhance osteogenic differentiation of human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) by activating the BMP/Smad signaling pathway. The negatively charged GQD‾ was then encapsulated in gelatin methacrylamide (GelMA) hydrogel via a UV light-activated photopolymerization in the presence of photoinitiator, obtaining an 3D injectable GQD‾/GelMA hydrogel scaffold. A significantly accelerated bone regeneration induced by GQD‾/GelMA hydrogel scaffolds was observed in the mouse calvarial defect model. The systematic study of the effects of surface charge on the stem cell behavior could provide much inspiration for the tissue engineering application of nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:para: Large-area bone defects, caused by abnormalities, trauma, tumor resection, infections, or senility, are an increasingly serious health problem [1,2], for which current reconstruction and repair of bone defects remain a clinical challenge. Autologous bone grafts are considered as the current gold standard treatment for bone defects due to the requirement of histocompatibility and bone conductivity, however, the clinical application of autografts is limited by donor supply, inflammatory risks, chronic pain, and high medical expenses [3,4]. Therefore, enormous research efforts have been devoted to seeking new kinds of bioactive materials to replace autografts, accelerating osteogenic differentiation and bone regeneration. Currently, various nanomaterials have been synthesized to promote the differentiation of human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs), such as metal nanoparticles [5], silica [6,7], natural or synthetic polymers [8–10], and carbon-based nanomaterials [11–13]. Although these nanomaterials have shown great potential in regulating the fate of stem cells, the investigations on the interaction between hMSCs and nanomaterials are limited and inexhaustive. Moreover, the relationship between the structure characteristics of nanomaterials and their ability to promote osteogenic differentiation remain largely unknown.

A number of studies have investigated the effects of nanomaterials on cell behaviors, revealing that wettability [14,15], morphology [16], and elasticity [17] of nanomaterials can individually or synergistically influence osteoblastic proliferation and differentiation. Besides, surface charge polarity is another important factors that endow nanomaterials with special physiochemical and biological properties, exerting significant influence on hMSC differentiation [18–24]. For example, Rapuano et al. reported positively charged titanium alloy can promote osteoblastic proliferation and mineralized bone deposition [24]. On the contrary, some scientists repeatedly confirmed that negatively charged nanoparticles could improve bone regeneration by upregulating the osteogenic gene expression [18,19]. These studies had been proved that the surface charge of various nanomaterials could influence osteoblastic differentiation, however, in the above cases, the underlying mechanism is still controversial and the surface charge was not the only variable. In addition, little attention has focused on developing a simplified model for single-factor study to verify the effect of surface charge on osteoblastic differentiation.

As a zero-dimensional (0D) carbon-based nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) not only inherit the unique physical, chemical and mechanical properties of 2D graphene, but also possess outstanding optical properties [25–28], excellent biocompatibility [29,30], as well as tunable surface functionalities [31,32], bringing a wide range of promising applications in bioimaging [33–35], biosensing [36,37], drug delivery [38], antibacterial [39], and cancer therapy [40–42]. Recently, the application of GQDs in biomedicine field has been extended to tissue engineering and regenerative medicine [43]. However, up to now, the application of GQDs in bone tissue engineering (BTE) still faces the following problems: Firstly, the possible interplay between the surface structural characteristic of GQDs and hMSCs is unclear. Secondly, the underlying molecular mechanism of GQD promoting osteoblastic differentiation has not been systematically studied.

In this work, we synthesized three types of edge-functionalized GQDs with different surface charge patterns through a rapid microwave-assisting reaction: positively charged GQDs (GQD + , +13.5 eV), nearly neutral GQDs (GQD +/ ‾, −4.7 eV), and strongly negatively charged GQDs (GQD‾, −15.2 eV), respectively. Choosing GQDs as the simplified model to solely investigate the effect of the surface charge on stem cell behavior is based on the consideration that GQDs with different functional groups such as carboxyl, amino, hydroxyl, or sulfo, could be readily synthesized by selecting appropriate precursors. As the surface functional group varied, the surface charge was the only variable while the other physicochemical properties such as surface chemistry, size, and morphology may not be altered, guaranteeing that GQDs could be as a simplified model for single-factor study to verify the effect of surface charge on osteoblastic differentiation. Furthermore, compared to previously reported titanium alloy [24] or ferroelectric crystal [18] employed to study the effect of surface charge on osteogenic differentiation, the biocompatible GQDs can be quickly cleared out of the body by the kidneys due to the small size, without inducing the long-term toxicity. Based on this situation, we systematically studied the influence of surface charge on the osteoblastic differentiation of GQDs, revealing that more negatively charged GQDs exhibited a more positive effect on self-renewal and enhanced osteogenic differentiation of hMSCs. Real-time quantitative PCR (RT-qPCR) and western blotting (WB) assays were performed to insightfully understand the molecular mechanisms, illustrating that the interplay between hMSCs and GQD could lead to the activation of BMP/Smad signaling pathway and the up-regulation of osteogenic-related genes and proteins. To further verify the enhanced osteogenic efficacy of GQD‾ in vivo, we constructed 3D injectable GQD‾/gelatin methacrylamide (GelMA) hydrogel composites through a one-step photo-polymerization reaction (Scheme 1 ). Micro computed tomography (Micro-CT) images demonstrated that GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels remarkably promoted bone tissue growth in the mouse cranial defect models.

A series of edge-functionalized GQDs with similar sizes but with different surface charges were controllably synthesized through an ultrafast microwave-assisted pyrolysis method using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) as the active monomers and inorganic salts or bases as the additive (Fig. 1 a). Briefly, the amine-functionalized GQDs with positive charge (GQD + ) and sulfo-functionalized GQDs with negative charge (GQD‾) were prepared from TNP with the addition of ammonia (NH3·H2O) or sodium sulfite (Na2SO3) at 200 °C for 5 min, respectively. To regulate the surface charge of GQDs, both NH3·H2O and Na2SO3 were added to the reaction system to obtain the nearly neutral amine, sulfo cofunctionalized GQDs (GQD +/‾). Morphological and crystallographic characteristics of as-synthesized GQDs were performed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). All of the three GQDs were nearly monodispersed with average sizes of 3.5 ± 0.9 (GQD+), 4.3 ± 1.1 (GQD +/‾), and 3.8 ± 1.4 nm (GQD‾), respectively (Fig. 1b). The similar average sizes and morphology of these GQDs guaranteed that GQDs could be as a simplified model for single-factor study to verify the effect of surface charge on osteoblastic differentiation. The lattice spacing of these GQDs determined by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were 0.21 nm (Fig. 1c), corresponding to the (100) plane of graphene [44,45]. Fig. 3a revealed that the zeta potential of GQD + , GQD +/‾, and GQD‾ in water at the same concentration (100 μg mL−1) was 13.5, −4.7, and −15.2 mV, respectively, suggesting that the surface charge of edge-functionalized GQDs can be readily regulated as the surface functional group varied. These results forcefully confirmed that we have successfully synthesized three types of GQDs with similar sizes but different surface charges by changing the additives in the reaction systems.

The optical properties of the three kinds of GQDs were given in Fig. 2
. A typical broad absorption peak around 390, 410, and 400 nm can be detected in the absorption spectra of GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾, respectively (Fig. 2a), which is ascribed to the contribution of different surface functional group [46,47]. Under UV light irradiation, the GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾ aqueous solutions emitted bright blue, cyan, and green fluorescence, respectively (inset in Fig. 2a). At the optimal PL excitation (PLE) wavelength of 430, 490, and 470 nm, the PL emission peak of GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾ were located at 480, 540, and 520 nm with a quantum yield of 33%, 46%, and 39%, respectively (Fig. 2a). Owing to the monodispersed size, the three kinds of GQDs exhibited excitation-independent PL behavior under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2b). Moreover, these GQDs exhibited outstanding fluorescence stability, where no significant reduction of the PL intensity can be detected after the 6-h continuous UV exposure (Fig. 2c). Besides, the fluorescence intensity of GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾ aqueous solution did not exhibit obvious change in acid and alkaline conditions (Fig. 2d), further illustrating the excellent photostability of these GQDs.

To further reveal their structural properties, we utilized X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern, Raman spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XRD pattern of all dried GQDs exhibited a broad diffraction peak at around 26° (Fig. 3b), corresponding to a larger (002) layer spacing of a graphite-like structure.[48] Raman spectra of GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾ at excitation of 532 nm showed that the intensity of the ordered G peak is significantly larger than that of the disordered D peak with a large IG/ID of 1.09, 1.03, and 1.14, respectively (Fig. 3c), indicating that the three kinds of GQDs have a high graphitic degree and similar structure.

We then investigated the surface functional groups of GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾ by the FT-IR and XPS spectra. For the FT-IR spectrum of GQD‾, two typical peaks at 1050 and 612 cm−1 could be ascribed to the bending vibration of -SO3 and C-S (Fig. 3d), confirming the existence of sulfo group. XPS results of GQD‾ in Fig. S1 further verified the presence of sulfo group. The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. S1a) exhibited three typical peaks of C 1 s, S 2p, and O 1 s with the content of 43.94 at.%, 12.38 at.%, and 43.68 at.%, respectively. The high-resolution S 2p spectrum (Fig. S1c) revealed two peaks at 170.2 and 168.8 eV, which could be assigned to SO3/2p1/2 and SO3/2p3/2, respectively. Additionally, the formation of -SO3 group in the GQD‾ could be also detected in the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S1d), which exhibited a typical peak at 535.8 eV. For GQD+, the stretching vibration of N–H and C-N at 3155 and 1350 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum could be observed (Fig. 3e), suggesting the formation of amine group. For XPS spectra of GQD+, in addition to C 1 s (286 eV) and O 1 s (532 eV), a typical peak at 401 eV could be ascribed to N 1 s (Fig. S2a), illustrating the presence of nitrogen-containing groups. Additionally, N–H (400.3 eV) and C-N (285.7 eV) in the high-resolution N 1 s and C 1 s spectra could be detected (Fig. S2b,c), indicating the existence of NH2 group in the GQD+. For the FT-IR spectrum of GQD +/‾, two typical peaks at 1041 and 617 cm−1 could be ascribed to -SO3 and C-S, while those at 1404 and 3148 cm−1 could be assigned to C-N and N–H, respectively (Fig. 3f), verified that both -SO3 and –NH2 group were presented in GQD +/‾. XPS analyses of GQD +/‾ revealed four strong peaks of C 1 s, S 2p, O 1 s, and N 1 s (Fig. S3a), suggesting the existence of nitrogen- and sulfur-containing groups. Furthermore, from the high-resolution S 2p, O 1 s, and N 1 s spectra (Fig. S3c-e), we observed the typical peaks of SO3/2p3/2, SO3/2p1/2, SO3, C-N, and NH2 at 168.2, 169.8, 535.9, 285.8, and 401.8 eV, respectively, demonstrating the presence of -SO3 and –NH2 group in the GQD +/‾. Based on the FT-IR and XPS results, we could conclude that negatively charged -SO3 and positively charged NH2 groups were functionalized at the edge of GQD‾ and GQD+, respectively, while the -SO3 and –NH2 groups were co-functionalized at the edge of GQD +/‾ (Fig. 1a). By regulating the type of edge functional groups of GQDs of similar sizes, we achieved the precise control of their surface charge patterns, ensuring that GQDs can be used as a simplified model for single-factor study to verify the effect of surface charge on osteogenic differentiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and intracellular uptake of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Before investigating the effects of these GQDs in promoting hMSC osteogenic differentiation, we first evaluated their biocompatibility and cellular uptake behaviors. No oxidative stress can be detected in GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾ treated hMSCs (Fig. S4). Furthermore, MTT results revealed that no significant cytotoxicity of GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾ against hMSCs can be observed at a much higher concentration (200 μg mL−1) (Fig. S5), suggesting the excellent biocompatibility of these GQDs. We further investigated their cellular internalization by quantitative flow cytometry and confocal laser scanning microscopy by virtue of the excellent fluorescence properties of these GQDs without adding other staining agents. Fig. 2 showed the typical absorption (ABS), PL, and PL excitation (PLE) spectra of the as-prepared GQD‾, GQD+/‾, and GQD+, indicating that these GQDs can be excited by 405 and 488 nm laser. As depicted by flow cytometry results in Fig. S6, obvious fluorescence signal of hMSCs can be detected after incubation with GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾ for 2 h, illustrating the efficient and fast cellular uptake of these GQDs. Confocal images further verified the easy internalization of all GQDs with the excitation wavelength of 405 and 488 nm (Fig. S7). After incubating hMSCs with GQD‾, GQD +/‾, or GQD + , a bright fluorescence signal was observed in the cytoplasm, indicating that the these GQDs were internalized by the cells. Besides, the spindle-shaped cell morphology of hMSCs was also be observed in confocal images, demonstrating the insignificant cytotoxicity of all GQDs. These results indicated that the biocompatible GQDs can be easily internalized by hMSCs, which laid the foundation for the following in vitro and in vivo experiments.

Having confirmed the biocompatibility of all GQDs, we then investigated the effect of GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾ on the osteogenic differentiation of hMSCs. Fig. 4 e revealed that no significantly enhanced alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity in GQD+ cultured hMSCs can be detected, suggesting that positively changed GQD+ (Zeta potential: +13.5 mV) exerted no impact on osteogenic differentiation. In sharp contrast, an enhanced ALP activity of hMSCs after 7 days of treatment was observed in the GQD +/‾ group, illustrating that the osteogenic differentiation of hMSCs was enhanced by weakly negatively charged GQD +/‾ (Zeta potential: −4.7 mV). Significantly, the ALP activity of hMSCs in the strongly negatively charged GQD‾ group (Zeta potential: −15.2 mV) was prominently increased by 2.45 folds, which was higher than that of GQD +/‾ group (1.48 fold), indicating that the higher osteoinductivity can be achieved as the negative charge of GQDs increases. Because ALP is a key regulator of the early stage differentiation of hMSCs and gradually decreases with further differentiation into osteoblasts [49], reduced ALP activity was observed in the hMSCs after 14 days of GQD‾ treatment compared with that in the hMSCs after 7 days of GQD‾ treatment (Fig. 3e). These results were consistent with the previous report [50], in which the early expression of ALP was associated with deposition of bone type matrix, and the subsequent downregulation of ALP with concomitant upregulation of other more advanced osteoblastic markers has been shown to be associated with the mineralization of bone. Besides, alizarin red S (ARS) staining was further performed to verify the tendency in charge-dependent ALP activity by measuring the amount of mineralized matrix. As described in Fig. 4a-c, after the hMSCs were cultured with different edge-functionalized GQDs for 14 days, GQD +/‾ showed a slightly more calcium mineral nodule (red stained), whereas GQD‾ group revealed a dramatically more nodule than others. Similarly, the quantitative ARS assay (Fig. 4d) also confirmed that hMSCs in the GQD‾ group exhibited a more calcium nodule deposition than that of other groups. The ALP activity and ARS staining results together clearly demonstrated that negative changed GQD‾ possessed a higher osteogenic potential than that of near neutral GQD +/‾ and positive changed GQD+.

Recently, numerous researches have demonstrated that two main signaling pathways are involved in governing the osteogenic differentiation of hMSCs, including bone morphogenic protein (BMP) and wingless-type MMTV integration site (Wnt) signaling [51–54]. Specifically, BMP signaling regulates osteogenic differentiation mainly through two pathways: one is the Smad-dependent pathway, and the other is non-Smad-dependent MAPK pathway [55,56]. The activation of MAPK or Smad signaling pathway can upregulate downstream osteogenic-related genes and proteins, including COL-1, OCN, and RUNX2. Therefore, we first assessed the downstream osteospecific gene and protein expression from hMSCs cultured in osteogenic media with PBS, GQD+, GQD +/‾, and GQD‾ treatment for 14 days using RT-qPCR and WB. As expected, the RT-qPCR results in Fig. S8 showed that the GQD +/‾ and GQD‾ both up-regulated the mRNA expression level of COL-1, OCN, and RUNX2 as compared to the control group. Significantly, these osteogenic-related proteins exhibited the highest expression level in GQD‾ treated hMSCs (Fig. 5 a), indicating that the effects of negative charged GQD‾ on promoting osteogenic differentiation were greater than that of positively charged GQD+ and near neutral GQD +/‾, which is consistent with the ALP activity and ARS staining results.

To further investigate the underlying mechanisms of enhanced osteogenic differentiation induced by negatively charged GQD‾, we evaluated the expression of osteogenic-related proteins involved in Wnt/β-catenin and BMP signaling pathways. Fig. 5a revealed that GQD‾ exhibited slight positive effects on the expression of β-catenin, suggesting that Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway was not dominant. Interestingly, up-regulated expression of BMP2 was observed in the presence of GQD‾ during the enhanced osteogenic differentiation. Moreover, the expression of phosphorylated Smad1/5 (p-Samd1/5) in GQD‾ group was remarkably up-regulated compared to other groups, but GQD‾ had no significant effect on the expression of phosphorylated ERK and P38 (Fig. 5b), suggesting that the BMP/Smad signaling pathway played a vital role in promoting osteogenesis effects of GQD‾. To further examine whether the BMP/Smad signaling pathway is involved in the GQD‾-hMSC interactions, WB analyses were carried out in the presence of noggin, the antagonist of the BMP/Smad signaling pathway. The results revealed that noggin decreased the p-Samd1/5 levels enhanced by GQD‾ (Fig. S9a,b), further verifying that the BMP/Smad signaling pathway was activated in hMSC differentiation upon GQD‾ treatment. Consistently, the ALP activity of hMSCs enhanced by GQD‾ was decreased in the presence of noggin (Fig. S9c). Based on these above results, we speculated that the interplay between positively charged BMP and negatively charged GQD‾ induced increased expression of BMP, and then promoted Smad1/5 phosphorylation. The complex formed by phosphorylated Smad1/5 and Smad4 then translocated into the nucleus of hMSCs, where they promoted the up-regulation of osteogenesis-related genes and proteins (Fig. 5c).

Having confirmed the excellent biocompatibility and osteoinductivity of negatively charged GQD‾ in vitro, we further evaluated its osteogenic performances in 3D scaffolds in a mouse calvarial defect model. Recently, naturally derived injectable hydrogels, such as hyaluronic acid, collagen, gelatin, and chitosan, have been used as a scaffold material for bone tissue engineering due to their tailorable physicochemical properties, high water content, and structural similarity to natural extracellular matrices (ECM) [57–59]. Particularly, gelatin methacrylamide (GelMA) with many methacrylamide groups can easily form covalently crosslinked hydrogels under a photo-initiated radical polymerization reaction [60,61]. Inspired by this, we propose to fabricate a 3D GQD‾/GelMA hydrogel scaffold by using a UV light-activated photopolymerization, by which negatively charged GQD‾ was incorporated in the porous hydrogel system and slowly released at the bone defect area to promote bone regeneration in situ. To the best of our knowledge, in vivo activation of osteogenic differentiation has not been reported using GQDs encapsulated in injectable GelMA hydrogels.

The GQD‾-modified GelMA hydrogels were fabricated via the photopolymerization of GQD‾, photoinitiator (Irgacure 2959), and GelMA aqueous solution under the UV light irradiation for 5 min (Fig. 6 a). Compared to the GelMA hydrogels with white color, the as-prepared GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels exhibited a brown color. Moreover, the deeper brown color of GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels was observed as the concentration of GQD‾ increased from 100 to 300 μg mL−1 (Fig. 6b), suggesting the successful encapsulation of GQD‾. Notably, the obvious color change of hydrogels could help us directly observe the dynamic release of GQD‾ from the hydrogels. SEM characterization was employed to investigate the surface morphology of GQD‾ incorporated GelMA hydrogels. As presented in Fig. 6c, a representative porous sponge-like structure was observed in GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels, demonstrating that the adequate nutrient supply for hMSC proliferation and differentiation could be achieved by the excellent water absorption capacity of porous hydrogel scaffold. We then investigated the water absorption capacity of GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels by measuring the swelling ratios and kinetics.The swelling rate of GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels in DI water was slightly higher than that of GelMA hydrogels without GQD‾ (Fig. 6d), indicating that the water absorption capacity of GelMA hydrogels was improved by encapsulating GQD‾, as a result of the excellent hydrophilicity of GQD‾. Furthermore, no significant change of the swelling ratios was detected when GQD‾/GelMA hydrogel immersed in different solutions (PBS, DI water, or DMEM) (Fig. S10a). Besides, the suitable degradation rate of hydrogel scaffold and formation rate of new bone-tissue is crucial to the bone tissue engineering. After immersing in PBS, DI water, or DMEM solution for 25 days, the remaining mass of GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels decreased to about 30% (Fig. 6e), verified the suitable biodegradation capacity of the hydrogel scaffolds. As a scaffold for bone regeneration in vivo, the mechanical property of GelMA hydrogels with or without GQD‾ should be evaluated using an Instron 5944 testing system. As described in Fig. 6f, compared with GelMA hydrogels without GQD‾, the compression modulus of GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels exhibited a slight increase, suggesting that the incorporation of GQD‾ has a positive effect on the mechanical support capacity of the hydrogels. We also investigated the rheological properties of GelMA and GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels. The time-sweep sequence (Fig. S11a) showed that the GelMA and GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels completely formed under UV light crosslinking, indicating that the incorporation of GQD‾ did not affect the free radical polymerization. The storage moduli (G′) and loss moduli (G″) gradually increased with prolongation of the irradiation time and reached the modulus equilibrium at approximately 120 s. During the entire gelation process, the G′ values of the GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels were higher than those of GelMA hydrogels, which is consistent with the mechanical results. From the results of the oscillatory frequency sweep of the hydrogels (Fig. S11b), no obvious undulation can be detected in the G′ and G″ values of GelMA and GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels as the angular frequency change, suggesting the good stability of these hydrogels.

Having evaluated the water absorption capacity, degradation rate, and mechanical property of GQD‾ encapsulated GelMA hydrogels, we next investigated the in vitro biocompatibility of GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels using the live/dead staining. Calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) were employed to stain the live (green fluorescence) and dead (red fluorescence) cells, respectively. The hMSCs were cultured on GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels for 1, 4, and 7 days. Fluorescent images in Fig. S12 revealed that adequate proliferation of hMSCs was observed in the GQD‾/GelMA hydrogel group, confirming their excellent biocompatibility. ALP activity and ARS staining were further carried out to evaluate the osteogenic activities of GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels. As depicted in Fig. S10b, the ALP activity of the hMSCs cultured on the GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels was higher than that of GelMA hydrogels without GQD‾. Similar to the ALP results, the calcium nodule deposition in GQD‾/ GelMA hydrogel group was remarkably higher than that of GelMA hydrogel group after the 14-day treatment (Fig. 6g), suggesting that the modification of GelMA hydrogels by negatively charged GQD‾ could enhance the osteogenic differentiation of hMSCs in the 3D scaffold.

We then assessed the positive effect of the GQD‾-modified hydrogel scaffolds on bone repair by injecting the GelMA aqueous solution with the addition of GQD‾ and photoinitiator into the defect site of the in vivo mouse calvarial defect model, immediately followed by the UV light irradiation for 1 min. Micro-CT was performed to evaluate the osteogenesis efficiency of GelMA hydrogels with or without GQD‾ after the 4-week implantation. After 4 weeks, the bone defects were healed nearly completely by implanted GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels (Fig. 6h), while GelMA hydrogel implantation in the absence of GQD‾ only showed a slightly positive effect. The quantitative results further revealed that the percentage of new bone area in the GQD‾/GelMA hydrogel group was calculated to be 94.6 ± 3.5% (Fig. S13), which was much higher than that of GelMA hydrogels group (8.4 ± 2.1%), confirming that the enhanced bone regeneration was achieved by the incorporation of negatively charged GQD‾.

In summary, we demonstrate that the surface charge of nanoparticles plays an important role on improving osteogenesis of hMSCs by using edge-functionalized GQDs as a biocompatible biomaterial. In vitro and in vivo osteogenic differentiation studies proved that the negatively charged GQD‾ could remarkably enhance bone regeneration by activating the BMP/Smad signaling pathway, and improving the expression levels of downstream osteogenic-related genes such as COL-1, OCN, and RUNX2. Furthermore, a biodegradable 3D GQD‾/GelMA hydrogel scaffold is successfully constructed through UV light photopolymerization, in which negatively charged GQD‾ was incorporated in the porous hydrogel system and slowly released at the bone defect area to promote bone regeneration in situ. Micro-CT images demonstrated that the GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels remarkably accelerated the osteogenic differentiation and bone regeneration in vivo after implantation for 30 days. Collectively, our study provided a simplified model of nanoparticles to investigate the effects of their surface charge on stem cell behavior by regulating the surface functional group of GQDs, which could help us deeply understand the crucial role of nanomaterial surface potential on osteogenic differentiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of different surface charged GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) was first synthesized from 4 g pyrene (TCI, Japan) in 320 mL of nitric acid at 70 °C for 24 h. 1 L of DI water was then employed to dilute the mixture. We then used a 0.22 nm microporous membrane to filtrate the mixture and obtain the TNP. For the preparation of GQD‾ or GQD + , TNP (0.025 g) with the addition of Na2SO3 (0.25 g) or NH3·H2O (2 mL) was dispersed in DI water (10 mL) and the microwave reaction was performed at 180 °C for 5 min. For the preparation of GQD +/‾, both NH3·H2O (2 mL) and Na2SO3 (0.25 g) were added to the TNP aqueous solution (10 mL) and the microwave reaction was performed at the same condition. After the microwave reaction, the product was dialyzed for 48 h to obtain the pure GQD‾, GQD +/‾, and GQD + .

GelMA was synthesized following previous report [62]. Briefly, 5 g of Type A porcine skin gelatin was dispersed into DI water (50 mL), followed by the addition of 4.0 mL methacrylic anhydride under the continuous stirring for 2 h. The GelMA solution was dialyzed against DI water using 12–14 kDa cutoff dialysis tubing for 7 days to remove salts and methacrylic acid. The dialyzed GelMA solution was then lyophilized for 7 days to generate a white porous foam and stored at −80 °C until further use. The GQD‾/GelMA hydrogel scaffolds were fabricated by an UV light-activate photopolymerization of GelMA and GQD‾ in the presence of photoinitiator (Irgacure 2959, Sigma, USA). Particularly, 15% (w/v) GelMA, GQD‾ (300 μg mL−1), and 0.17% (w/v) Irgacure 2959 were fully dissolved in DI water, followed by a UV light irradiation (365 nm, 400 W) for 5 min.

TEM images of GQD + , GQD +/‾, and GQD‾ were measured by JEM-2100F microscope (Hitachi, Japan). XRD patterns were obtained with an D/max-2550 (Rigaku, Japan). FT-IR spectra of GQD + , GQD +/‾, and GQD‾ were investigated by a Nicolet AVATAR 370 FT-IR (Thermo, USA). We utilized a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) to investigate the XPS characterization of GQD + , GQD +/‾, and GQD‾. The Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, England) was employed to measure the particle size and zeta potential of GQD + , GQD +/‾, and GQD‾. UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained by Hitachi 3100 and 7000 spectrophotometer (Japan), respectively.

For SEM characterization, GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels were first freeze-dried at −60 °C for 48 h and then placed on an aluminum foil. After sputtering with gold for 1 min, the GQD‾/GelMA hydrogel samples were examination by the SEM (S-4800, Hitachi, Japan). For the swelling kinetic characterization, the dry GelMA or GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels were weighed before immersed in 5 mL of DI water at 37 °C. At each measurement time point, the excess surface water of the samples was removed before weighing GelMA or GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels. The degradation rate of GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels was assessed in three kinds of solution, including PBS, DI water, or DMEM. The GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels were immersed in a solution (10 mL) for 600 h. Then, at the different time point, we washed the GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels for three times using the DI water and weighed the hydrogels to calculate the percent mass remain. We further used an Instron 5944 testing system to evaluate the mechanical properties of GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels according to the previous report [60]. The rheological characteristics of the hydrogels were measured by using a rotational rheometer (DHR, TA Instruments, USA) equipped with a UV crosslinking instrument. 0.5 mL of GelMA or GQD‾/GelMA mixture was first dropped onto a quartz plate. A time sweep of the solution was then performed with a strain of 1.0% under UV light irradiation. The completely gelled hydrogel on the plates was further used to conduct a frequency sweep at a constant strain of 1% while varying the frequency from 0.1 to 100 rad/s.

hMSCs were purchased from Cyagen Biosciences (Suzhou, China) and cultured on 6-well plates in the presence of basal medium in the presence of fetal bovine serum (FBS, 10%, v/v). MTT assay was used to evaluate the biocompatibility of GQD‾, GQD +/‾, and GQD + against hMSCs. Briefly, hMSCs were seeded in the 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h. These GQDs with varied concentrations (12.5, 25, 50, 100 and 200 μg mL−1) were added into the wells and cultured for 24 or 48 h, respectively. 20 μL of MTT was added into the wells for another 4 h incubation. Finally, 150 μL of DMSO was added to replace the medium and the cell viabilities of these GQDs against hMSCs were measured by a Microplate Reader (Bio-Rad, USA).

The quantitative flow cytometry was used to investigate the cellular uptake of GQD‾, GQD +/‾, and GQD + . Briefly, hMSCs were first seeded on 24-well plates. After incubating for 24 h, these GQDs at the concentration of 100 μg mL−1 were added into the wells and incubated for 2 h at 37 °C. Finally, we used a flow cytometer (Beckman, USA) to measure the fluorescence intensity of these GQDs.

The cell imaging of GQD‾, GQD +/‾, and GQD + on hMSCs was observed by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Olympus, Japan). Briefly, Approximate 1 × 105 hMSCs were first seeded in confocal small dish and cultured for 24 h. These GQDs at the concentration of 50 μg mL−1 were then added to the dish and incubated for another 2 h. Finally, hMSCs were examined under CLSM with the excitation wavelength of 405 and 488 nm.

The DCFH-DA probe (Beyotime, China) was employed to detect the ROS level of GQD‾, GQD +/‾, or GQD + (100 μg mL−1) treated hMSCs. 5 × 104 hMSCs were first seeded into the 6-well plates and incubated for 24 h. These GQDs were then added to the wells and incubated for another 24 h·H2O2 (200 mM) treated hMSCs were used as the positive controls. Finally, we used a fluorescence microscope (Olympus BX53, Japan) to evaluate the ROS levels of these samples.

The live/dead cell staining assay was performed to investigate the in vitro cytotoxicity of the GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels. 2 × 105 hMSCs were seeded on GelMA or GQD‾/GelMA hydrogel scaffolds in the 6-well plates and incubated for 7 days at 37 °C. At days 1, 4, and 7, the viability of hMSCs was evaluated by the live/dead staining kit using calcein-AM and PI to stain live (green fluorescence) and dead (red fluorescence) cells, respectively. Fluorescent images were captured by a confocal laser scanning microscope (Olympus, Japan). ALP activity assay and ARS staining were then preformed to confirm the enhanced osteogenic differentiation of hMSCs induced by these samples. For ALP activity assays, 2 × 105 hMSCs were first seeded into the 6-well plates and incubated for 48 h. GQD‾, GQD +/‾, and GQD + at the same concentration of 100 μg mL−1 were then added to the wells in the presence of osteogenic induction medium for 14 days, respectively. The medium was refreshed every 3 days. These samples treated hMSCs were harvested and lysed after the incubation of 7 or 14 days. The supernatant was collected through the centrifugation of the lysed samples. Then, the total amount of intracellular protein was detected *through a BCA protein assay kit (Solarbio, China). Finally, we employed the ALP assay kit (Beyotime, China) to evaluate the ALP activity of these sample cultured hMSCs according to manufacturer’s instruction. For ARS staining, after the incubation of these samples for 14 days, 4% paraformaldehyde (Beyotime, China) and 2% alizarin red (Sigma, USA) were employed to fix and stain the hMSCs, respectively. Then, hMSCs were imaged by a fluorescence microscope (Olympus BX53, Japan). Besides, the quantitative results of ARS staining were also carried out by measuring the 570 nm absorbance of hMSCs after the addition of cetylpyridium chloride (10%).

MiniBEST RNA extraction kit (Takara, Japan) was employed to extract the total RNA from GQD‾, GQD +/‾, or GQD + treated hMSCs. Then, cDNA was synthesized from the total RNA (1 μg) using the PrimeScript reagent kit (Takara, Japan). Finally, RT-qPCR measurement was carried out with cDNA (2 μL) in a reaction system (20 μL) using the TB green premix ex taq (Takara, Japan). We listed the sequences of the used primers in our work:
ALP forward primer: 5′-AACACCACCCAGGGGAAC-3′;
ALP reverse primer: 5′-GGTCACAATGCCCACAGATT-3′;
RUNX2 forward primer: 5′-GGCCCACAAATCTCAGATCGTT-3′;
RUNX2 reverse primer: 5′-CACTGGCGCTGCAACAAGAC-3′;
OCN forward primer: 5′-GGCGCTACCTGTATCAATGG-3′;
OCN reverse primer: 5′-TCAGCCAACTACTCACAGTC-3′;
COL-1 forward primer: 5′-GAGGGCCAAGACGAAGACATC-3′;
COL-1 reverse primer: 5′-CAGATCACGTCATCGCACAAC-3′;
GAPDH: forward primer: 5′-ATGGGAAGCTGGTCATCAAC-3′;
GAPDH reverse primer: 5′-GGATGCAGGGATGATGTTCT-3′.

hMSCs treated with GQD‾, GQD +/‾, or GQD + for 7 days were first lysed in cold RIPA cell lysis buffer in the presence of PMSF (1 mM) for 0.5 h. 20 µg of extractive protein were separated by 12% SDS-PAGE electrophoresis and transferred to PVDF membranes (Beyotime, China). We then employed BSA (5%) to block the PVDF membranes for 2 h, followed by incubating with primary antibodies at 4 °C for 24 h. The used antibodies in this work were listed as following: anti-RUNX2 (Cst, 12556), Anti-BMP2 (Abcam, ab14933), anti-Smad (Cst, 6944 T), anti-p-SMAD (Cst, 9516 T), anti-COL-1 (Abcam, Ab90395), anti-β-catenin (Cst, 8480), Anti-p-ERK (Abcam, ab79483), anti-OCN (Abcam, Ab133612), and anti-GAPDH (Abcam, Ab8245, 1:5000). After incubating, the PVDF membranes were further incubated with the secondary antibody (HRP-linked goat anti-rabbit IgG, Jackson, 115–035-003, 1:5000). Finally, the protein was visualized in the ECL detection systems.

All animal experiments were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Shanghai University. 6 week old Balb/c mice were operated under anesthesia by using pentobarbitalsodium under aseptic conditions. A craniotomy defect of 6 mm diameter was created byusing a drill bit. 100 μL GelMA with or without GQD‾ in the presence of photoinitiator were injected into the craniotomy defect (three mice per group). GQD‾/GelMA hydrogels were formed under UV light irradiation for 1 min. Micro-CT was performed to evaluate osteogenesis efficiency at day 0 and 30.

All the experiments were carried out for three times and the data were analyzed by using SPSS software (SPSS 18.0, USA). All the data were presented as mean ± SD. The statistical differences were analyzed by a paired Student’s t test; p values<0.05 were considered to indicate statistical differences.
